questions.question: What does RTO primarily describe in a regional failure plan?
options: How long it takes to restore service after the outage begins.
How much data each request may store.
How many regions the company can afford.
How many engineers are on call.
questions.explanation: RTO is the recovery time objective: the time budget to restore service.

questions.question: Why is RPO a separate design concern from RTO?
options: Because you can restore service quickly while still losing more recent data than the business accepts.
Because RPO only matters for logging systems.
Because low RTO automatically guarantees zero data loss.
Because RPO replaces the need for replication.
questions.explanation: Time to recover and acceptable data loss are related but distinct promises.

questions.question: What usually sets a lower bound on real-world RTO?
options: Detection, classification, traffic cutover, and target-region readiness work.
Only the syntax of the health-check endpoint.
The number of dashboards in the NOC.
Whether the service uses JSON or protobuf.
questions.explanation: Recovery time includes more than a routing change.

questions.question: Why is failover flapping dangerous?
options: Repeated cutovers can amplify instability and make a partial incident much worse.
It permanently lowers storage cost.
It improves user cache locality automatically.
It only affects testing environments.
questions.explanation: Oscillating cutovers can compound outages and create inconsistent state.

questions.question: Which signal is most important before promoting a new write region?
options: Replication freshness and confidence that the target region can accept authoritative writes.
Whether the region name is alphabetically first.
Whether CDN cache hit ratio is above 90 percent.
Whether operators recently rotated passwords.
questions.explanation: Promotion safety depends on the target region's data readiness.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with degraded read-only mode during recovery?
options: It preserves partial availability but delays full write functionality.
It increases write throughput immediately.
It removes the need for replication metrics.
It guarantees zero customer impact.
questions.explanation: Read-only mode is a deliberate compromise to shorten recovery.

questions.question: Which observability signal best verifies the recovery plan itself?
options: Measured drill RTO and RPO compared with the stated objectives.
Only a monthly count of deploys.
Average HTML payload size.
Whether a wiki page exists.
questions.explanation: Drills prove whether the plan meets the promise.

questions.question: What is the strongest interview answer when asked about failover automation?
options: Use automation with guardrails, explicit promotion gates, and a limited manual emergency path.
Automate everything with no rollback path.
Keep everything manual so humans stay in the loop for every decision.
Avoid documenting who is allowed to trigger failover.
questions.explanation: The best answers balance speed with safety and auditable control.
